ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a prepared crab dish in spicy sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Singapore/Malaysian chilli crab. A0CJW (cooked crustaceans) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chilli crab as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving is one whole mud crab with sauce, weighing approximately 500 grams, typically shared or consumed as a main course at a seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Chilli Crab / Crab in Chilli Sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chilli crab / crab in chilli sauce used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 150 kcal/100g, protein 12g, fat 9g, carbohydrate 6g, sodium 500mg). Minor micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1. Trans fat (0.04g/100g) retained from input as a plausible trace value for a stir-fried dish using commercial sauces; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in HPB for this dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood dish with tomato-based sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore chilli crab. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed seafood dishes with tomato-based sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is low (6g/100g), predominantly from tomato paste, oyster sauce, and added sugar. Glycemic load per 500g serving = (45 × 30g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.5; however, per standard 100g reference portion GL = (45 × 6) / 100 = 2.7. Serving GL reported for the 100g reference portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 500mg per 100g — exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chilli paste in the sauce base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200g of crab with sauce, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce from the kitchen, and avoid soaking mantou or bread in the sauce, which substantially increases sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6g/100g) and low GI (~45); high protein (12g/100g) from crab supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli crab is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without fried mantou or white rice; the dish itself has a low glycaemic load, but pairing with high-GI accompaniments will significantly raise the overall meal glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (500mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (70mg/100g); saturated fat is low at 1.6g/100g and the dish is not deep-fried
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the primary concern is sodium load rather than fat content; a single 500g serving delivers approximately 2500mg sodium, which approaches or exceeds the daily recommended limit, so portion control and sauce reduction are strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mud crab provides omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein with anti-inflammatory potential; however, high sodium and nightshade-derived ingredients (tomato, chilli) may aggravate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of mud crab but should monitor for flares related to nightshade sensitivity (tomato, chilli) and request reduced sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Crab is one of Singapore's and Malaysia's most iconic seafood dishes, consisting of whole mud crabs (typically Sri Lankan or local mud crab, Scylla serrata) cooked in a rich, semi-thick, sweet-savoury-spicy tomato-based sauce. The sauce is built from a base of blended chilli paste, garlic, ginger, tomato paste, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sugar, finished with beaten egg stirred through to create silky ribbons throughout the sauce. The dish is stir-fried and then simmered to allow the crab to absorb the sauce flavours. Texture is a contrast between firm, sweet crab meat and the glossy, slightly sticky sauce. It is traditionally served with deep-fried or steamed mantou (Chinese buns) for dipping. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 12g protein, 9g fat (of which 1.6g is saturated), 6g carbohydrate, 3g sugar, 0.6g dietary fibre, and 500mg sodium. The high sodium content (500mg/100g) makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The dish is not halal-certified in most restaurant settings due to the use of oyster sauce and potential cross-contamination. It is a major cultural symbol of Singaporean cuisine and is commonly served at seafood restaurants as a shared main course for 2–4 persons.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chilli crab / crab in chilli sauce and are internally coherent (macros sum correctly, sodium is plausible for a soy/oyster sauce-based dish). However, the original input provenance was 'ai_generated' from a delivery platform, and no direct lab analysis or exact HPB record URL was available. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chilli crab is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for chilli crab as of 2024. trans_fat_g retained at 0.04g/100g as a plausible trace value for a stir-fried dish using commercial sauces; this is not confirmed zero but a trace level consistent with commercial sauce ingredients. The input listed two portions: 1 whole crab (~500g) and 2 whole crabs (~1500g); the default serving used for GL calculation is 500g (1 whole crab). The dish is described as 'Malaysian-style' in the input but chilli crab is widely recognised as a Singaporean national dish with Malaysian heritage; both attributions are retained in cuisine and region fields. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed seafood dishes with tomato-based sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chilli crab was found in the approved GI source database.
